candidateFor: Patients aged 40+ seeking preventive health screening with comprehensive diagnostics in a single visit
International patients traveling from Africa, GCC, or Southeast Asia for preventive care
Individuals wanting cancer detection workup at 40-70% lower cost than Western countries
Patients requiring English-language medical reports and international medical records
Those combining screening with leisure travel (outpatient same-day procedure allows tourism)
Patients with family history of cancer seeking early detection before symptoms

whatToExpect.detail: Speak with a screening coordinator about your health history and which tests suit your age/risk profile. You will receive fasting instructions (typically 6-8 hours before endoscopy). Arrive early for check-in and to review consent forms with English-language support.

Endoscopy (gastroscopy and colonoscopy) uses light sedation for comfort; most other tests—chest CT, ultrasound, tumor-marker blood panels, and often PET-CT—require no anesthesia. You will be walked through each station by medical staff. Many centers provide preliminary findings before discharge.

whatToExpect.duration: Same-day discharge; 3-5 days recommended in Korea
whatToExpect.detail: Resume normal activity immediately. Avoid driving on the day of sedation. Comprehensive written report with images is typically ready within 24-48 hours. Schedule a follow-up consultation to review results with a physician and discuss any next steps or referrals.

whyKorea: South Korea holds a world-leading reputation in endoscopy technology and preventive screening protocols, backed by strict MFDS regulatory standards
Comprehensive packages cost approximately $900–$2,500 USD, roughly 40-70% less than equivalent screening in the United States, United Kingdom, or Australia
English-language medical reports, imaging, and physician consultations are standard at international screening centers, removing language barriers for overseas patients
Same-day outpatient screening allows you to complete diagnostics during a short trip, with results ready before you depart

faqs.question: Is sedation used during cancer screening in Korea?
faqs.answer: Light sedation is used only for the endoscopy portion (gastroscopy and colonoscopy) for comfort. Chest CT, ultrasound, blood work, and PET-CT require no anesthesia. You will be conscious during most of your appointment.

faqs.question: How long do I need to stay in Korea for screening?
faqs.answer: Three to five days is recommended. This allows time for pre-screening fasting, the full-day screening appointment, and a follow-up consultation to review results and imaging with a physician before you leave.

faqs.question: What happens if screening finds something abnormal?
faqs.answer: Your physician will discuss findings during your follow-up consultation. Additional imaging, specialist referral, or biopsy may be recommended. Many centers provide written summaries and imaging files you can take home or share with your physician abroad.

faqs.question: Can I travel or work the day after screening?
faqs.answer: Yes. Screening is a same-day outpatient procedure with no recovery period. You can resume normal activities immediately after discharge. Avoid strenuous exercise or driving only if you received sedation that morning.

faqs.question: How much does a comprehensive cancer screening package cost?
faqs.answer: Typical packages in Korea range from approximately $900 to $2,500 USD, depending on which tests are included (endoscopy, CT, ultrasound, tumor markers, and PET-CT). This is 40-70% less than comparable screening in Western countries.
